coredata.dc:title: Novel hybrids based on graphene quantum dots covalently linked to glycol corroles for multiphoton bioimaging

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess excellent optical properties, high photostability, aqueous solubility and bio-compatibility. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs surface and edges provides an excellent opportunity to explore them as anchoring units for covalent functionalization. In addition, the nonlinear optical response of GQDs allows the development of optical sensors operating in biological media. To evaluate the possibility of using GQDs to introduce a hydrophobic sensing unit inside animal cells and to add a nonlinear response to the sensing unit we prepared a novel hybrid based on GQDs covalently linked to corrole units bearing a glycol branch. Covalent functionalization was supported by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Both the height and the diameter of the hybrid showed distributions peaking below 4 nm. The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of the hybrids were additive with respect to that of the GQDs and the corrole. Insights about the most stable conformation of the corrole with respect to the GQDs core was provided by in silico studies using a model structure. The internalization and distribution of the hybrids in live animal cells was evaluated in human breast adenbocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7 cells) using confocal and multiphoton microscopy. Notably, multiphoton microscopy allowed for image collection using nonlinear excitation in the near-infrared and emission in the red part of the visible spectra.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthetic route used to prepare the glycol corroles 2a and 2b, and the hybrids GQD_2a. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FTIR spectra of GQDs (black), corrole 2a (red) and GQD_2a (blue). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Raman spectra of GQDs (black) and GQD_ 2a (blue). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TGA analyses under inert atmosphere of GQD_CO2H (black), GQD_2a (blue) and corrole 2a (red). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
High-resolution XPS spectra and deconvolution of C1s, N1s and F1s peaks for corrole 2a, GQDs and GQD_2a. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of GQDs and of the hybrid GQD_2a by TEM and AFM. (A) The TEM image and (B) the size distribution of GQDs shows that the diameter is centered at 3.5 nm. In GQD_2a the diameters are in the range of 2–6 nm with an average size of ∼3.8 nm (C and D). Cross-section of the AFM image (E). The AFM image shows that maximum height of the GQD_2a is 5.5 nm (F). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–vis absorption (A) and normalized emission spectra (B) of corrole 2a, GQDs and GQD_2a in DMSO when excited at 400 nm at 298 K. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

GNP_COOH (A), corrole 2a (B), corrole stacked on to the carbon core of the hybrid GNP_2a (C), and corrole alongside the carbon core in nearly co-planar arrangement, GNP_2a (D). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Simulated absorption spectra for the GNP_2a (stacked and side conformations) compared with that of the isolated GNP-COOH and corrole 2a. The spectra were computed in a water continuum and plotted using a Gaussian function with 1000 cm−1 FWHM to account for inhomogeneous broadening of the discrete spectral lines (similar results were obtained in the gas phase). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells incubated with GQD_2a and the nuclear stain Hoechst 33342. For each image plane (xy and xz), the emission of GQD_2a was collected upon excitation at 458 nm in two channels, one at 474–600 nm (green emission, panels A and E) corresponding to emission of the GQDs and the other at 600–730 nm (red emission, panels B and F) corresponding to emission of corrole 2a. The emission of the nuclear stain was collected at 400–470 nm upon excitation at 760 nm (blue emission, panels C and G). The overlays of the separated channels are shown in panels D and H for the xy, and xz planes, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence microscopy image of MCF-7 cells incubated with GQD_2a. Images A and B where recorded by collecting the emission in the 490–740 nm region upon excitation at 458 nm (OPE) and at 760 nm (TPE) respectively. Panel C compares the spectra observed in the cell culture with that recorded in DMSO solution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess excellent optical properties, high photostability, aqueous solubility and bio-compatibility. The presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on GQDs surface and edges provides an excellent opportunity to explore them as anchoring units for covalent functionalization. In addition, the nonlinear optical response of GQDs allows the development of optical sensors operating in biological media. To evaluate the possibility of using GQDs to introduce a hydrophobic sensing unit inside animal cells and to add a nonlinear response to the sensing unit we prepared a novel hybrid based on GQDs covalently linked to corrole units bearing a glycol branch. Covalent functionalization was supported by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Raman and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Both the height and the diameter of the hybrid showed distributions peaking below 4 nm. The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of the hybrids were additive with respect to that of the GQDs and the corrole. Insights about the most stable conformation of the corrole with respect to the GQDs core was provided by in silico studies using a model structure. The internalization and distribution of the hybrids in live animal cells was evaluated in human breast adenbocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7 cells) using confocal and multiphoton microscopy. Notably, multiphoton microscopy allowed for image collection using nonlinear excitation in the near-infrared and emission in the red part of the visible spectra.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have arisen as one of the most notable luminescent carbon nanomaterials [1–4]. GQDs possess distinctive electronic properties, they have high photoluminescence quantum yields and their emission is characterized by different fluorescence colors under varied excitation wavelengths [5,6]. Compared to traditional semiconductor QDs and organic dyes, GQDs are apparently superior in terms of biological properties. They exhibit high photostability, low toxicity, good aqueous solubility and bio-compatibility [7–10]. These properties have made GQDs interesting alternatives for applications in fluorescence bio-imaging, sensing, photocatalysis, water treatment, drug delivery and photodynamic therapy [11–14]. From the different synthetic procedures available to prepare GQDs (top-down and bottom-up approaches) [15], the top-down approach has the advantage to reduce the production of molecular side products, resulting in a high yield of GQDs with oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface and edges [16]. These functional groups, namely carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, enable covalent attachment, electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding with other suitable moieties affording materials with modified optical, chemical and electronic properties to be explored in a wide range of applications [17]. Tetrapyrrolic macrocycles like porphyrins and corroles can be particularly suited for conjugation with GQDs due to their unique chemical and photophysical properties. Despite this, not much is known about GQDs conjugated with porphyrins, and much less is known about conjugation with corroles.

Corroles are aromatic tetrapyrrolic macrocycles with a skeletal structure different from the porphyrins. In porphyrins the four units of pyrrole type are linked through four meso-carbon bridges, whereas in corroles two of those units present a direct pyrrole–pyrrole linkage. This structural difference results in a more π-electron rich system when compared to porphyrin, which confers to corrole distinct properties in terms of catalytic activity, sensing and photochemistry [18–20]. Corroles are efficient catalysts for oxidation and group transfer reactions and excellent chemosensors for ions [21–24]. In the therapeutic field, corroles have been explored as contrast agents for biomedical imaging, photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy of cancer and inactivation agents for bacteria, viruses, and fungi [25–28].

Conversely to GQDs, the conjugation of other types of carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene oxide (GO) and nanodiamonds to tetrapyrrolic macrocycles has been explored in catalysis, photocatalysis, sensing and biological assays [29–36]. The smaller size of the GQDs, their water solubility and high biocompatibility can be particularly advantageous for application in nanomedicine and in biomedical and biological research. Indeed, the conjugation of GQDs to porphyrins is a recent topic of research (Table S1). In one of the first reports on the synthesis of a nanocomposite based on GQDs and porphyrins, the authors referred that the hydrothermal treatment of the zinc(II) complex of 5,10,15,20-tetra(4-pyridyl)porphyrin (ZnPor) with GQDs afforded a material with an enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible-light irradiation when compared with the pure zinc porphyrin [37]. This combination of GQDs with the ZnPor caused a large red shift of the absorption spectrum, which was attributed to some mutual enrichment and interaction between the two light-emitting substances. Achadu et al. reported the immobilization of zinc(II) and cobalt(II) complexes of porphyrins and phthalocyanines derivatized with pyrene moieties on GQDs via a π-π stacking interaction [38]. These supramolecular hybrids were stable in dimethylformamide (DMF) owing to the strong π-π stacking interaction between the GQDs and the porphyrins. Significant changes in the optical properties of the hybrid with respect to the isolated components were observed upon conjugation. Another recent work reported the incorporation of a meso-diarylporphyrin with high singlet oxygen generation ability to (PEG)ylated and aptamer-functionalized GQDs giving access to a multifunctional theranostic agent. Among other features, the resultant hybrid exhibited good ability to discriminate cancer cells from normal cells [39].

Based on the above described work on porphyrins, and having in mind the unique properties of corroles, we envisaged that their conjugation with GQDs could lead to new nanocomposites with improved performance in biomedical applications.

Corroles are considered promising compounds for the development of theranostic agents, however their low solubility in aqueous solutions limits biomedical applications [25]. The coupling of corroles with water-soluble GQDs can be an efficient strategy to overcome solubility limitations and result in synergistic effects for the development of two-photon absorption nanoplatforms with potential as highly efficient two-photon donors in PDT [40]. We report, for the first time, a two-step methodology for the efficient covalent functionalization of GQDs with corrole macrocycles. First the corrole core was modified with glycol branches in order to increase their water solubility and simultaneously provide the active sites for the conjugation with GQDs. Subsequently, the covalent coupling of GQDs with corroles bearing glycol branches was successfully achieved via carbodiimide approach, through the formation of ester bonds. The covalent functionalization is confirmed by FTIR, Raman, XPS, UV–vis and electronic microscopy.

The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra, supported by electronic structure calculations, suggest that the corrole is aligned side-by-side with the carbon core. As a proof of concept on the high potential for biomedical applications of these graphene-based hybrid nanomaterials, their internalization and distribution in live animal cells was evaluated in cancer cell lines (MCF-7, human breast adenocarcinoma cell line) using fluorescence microscopy. Multiphoton images could be recorded by collecting emission in the red part of the visible spectra upon nonlinear excitation in the near-infrared (NIR). These results show that the resulting hybrids can be particularly useful as fluorescent labels for thick biological samples due to the reduced scattering and the longer penetration depth of NIR light.

The reagents used for the preparation of corroles and hybrid materials were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich: pyrrole, 2,3,4,5,6-pentafluorobenzaldehyde, 2,3-dichloro-5,6-dicyano-p-benzoquinone, trifluoroacetic acid, sodium hydroxide, [1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride] (EDAC), graphite flakes, hydrogen peroxide and ammonium hydroxide solution. The ethylene glycol was obtained from Alfa Aesar. All solvents were used as received from different commercial sources, except tetrahydrofuran that was dried using standard procedures.
Mass spectra (HRMS) were carried out at the University of Vigo (CACTI), Spain on an APEXQe FT-ICR (BrukerDaltonics, Billerica, MA) mass spectrometer in m/z (rel. intensity %), using CHCl3 as solvent.

1H, 19F and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on Bruker Avance 300 (at 300, 282 and 75 MHz, respectively) spectrometers in CDCl3. All chemical shifts are denoted in δ (ppm) and coupling constants (J) in Hertz (Hz). 1H assignments were further supported by 2D COSY trials (mixing time of 800 ms). For graphene quantum dots the 13C NMR spectrum was collected on a Bruker AVIII 700 MHz (175 MHz), using deuterated D2O.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed using a Hitachi H-9000 operating at 300 KV. The Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured using a Mattson 7000 FT-IR spectrometer, with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and 128 scans. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) were performed under air and nitrogen atmosphere, from RT to 600 °C, with a heating rate of 10 °C/min and recorded in a TA-TGAQ500 equipment. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using an Ultra High Vacuum (UHV) system. The HR-spectra of C1s, N1s and F1s were acquired with a pass-energy of 20 eV at normal emission take-off angle.

Raman spectra were recorded in NT-MDT-Microscope using laser light of 532 nm. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) in tapping mode was conducted using SPM Nanoscope IIIa multimode equipped with RTESPSS tip (Veeco).

A JASCO V-540 spectrophotometer and a Fluorolog-3 (Horiba) spectrofluorimeter were used to collect the absorption and emission spectra, respectively. The fluorescence quantum yields were determined relatively to a fluorescence standard. For corrole_2a, the luminescence quantum yield was determined by integrating the emission in the 600–750 nm (GQD) upon excitation at 550 nm and using a cresyl violet perchlorate solution in methanol (φ = 0.54) as reference [41]. For GQDs and GQD_2a the luminescence quantum yields were determined by integrating the emission in the 420–600 nm (GQD) and 420–750 nm (GQD_2a) upon excitation at 400 nm and using a solution of coumarin 153 in absolute ethanol (φ = 0.54) as reference. The quantum yield of the individual GQDs and corrole units upon conjugation were determined after deconvoluting the overall emission spectra into its components and correcting for the percentage of excitation light absorbed by each unit.

The Single-Photon Timing technique was used to measure the fluorescence decay under excitation at 340 nm (2nd harmonic of a Coherent Radiation Dye laser 700 series, dye DCM, 610–680 nm, 130 mW, 5 ps, 4 MHz). The emission was collected by a Jobin Yvon HR320 monochromator (Horiba Jovin Ivon Inc.) at the magic angle using. The instrument response functions (35–80 ps FWHM) was estimated using a scattering dispersion of colloidal silica in water. Decay curves were stored in 1024 channels with 24.2 ps per channel and an accumulation of 20k counts in the peak channel. The fluorescence decays were analyzed by a non-linear least-squares reconvolution method using the TRFA DP software by SSTC (Scientific Software Technologies Center, Belarusian State University, Minsk, Belarus).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots with additional carboxylic groups (GQD_CO2H)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GQDs with extra carboxylic groups (GQD_CO2H) required two steps: i) hydrothermal treatment of GO in the presence of NH4OH and H2O2 and ii) reaction of the GQDs obtained with chloroacetic acid to introduce additional carboxylic functions. The synthesis and characterization of the initial GQDs was described in detail in our previous paper [6]. Succinctly, a GO dispersion (12 mg/mL) was mixed with NH4OH/H2O2 (1:4 vol) and treated at 150 °C for 5 h in a Teflon lined autoclave. The products of the hydrothermal procedure were purified by filtration and dialysis and the GQDs collected by lyophilisation.
TGA: weight loss and temperature desorption (carboxylic acid groups): 22.0%, 200-600 °C.
Raman: 1602 cm−1 (G band), 1371 cm−1 (D band); ID/IG = 1.9.

To prepare the GQDs with extra carboxylic groups (GQD_CO2H), 0.80 g of chloroacetic acid and 0.60 g of NaOH were added to 0.080 g of GQDs in 30 mL of Millipore water followed by ultrasonication for 3 h to convert the –OH groups present in GQDs into –OCH2COO−. Then the neutralization with HCl (1 M) was performed to convert the salt groups into the acid groups. The carboxylated GQDs were dialysed during three days and dried by lyophilisation [42].
TGA: weight loss and temperature desorption (carboxylic acid groups): 48.5%, 200–650 °C.
Raman: 1608 cm−1 (G band), 1369 cm−1 (D band); ID/IG = 1.8.
The precursor 5,10,15-tris(pentafluorophenyl)corrole 1 was synthesized according to the procedure described in the literature [43].

A round-bottom flask, equipped with a magnetic stirring bar was charged with sodium hydride (15 mg, 0.66 mmol), ethylene glycol (560 mg, 12.5 mmol) and tetrahydrofuran (15 mL) under inert atmosphere (Ar). The mixture was refluxed for 30 min followed by addition of 5,10,15tris(pentafluorophenyl)corrole 1 (75 mg, 0.076 mmol) and tetrahydrofuran (15 mL). The resulting reaction mixture was heated at reflux until consumption of the starting corrole 1 (8 h). After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was diluted with chloroform and washed with water (3 × 20 mL). The organic phase was concentrated under vacuum and the residue was purified by silica gel preparative TLC using as eluent a mixture of chloroform-methanol (9:1). The corroles 2a and 2b were isolated, and crystallized in chloroform:methanol. The compounds were characterized by 1H and 19F NMR, UV–vis and MS (Figures S1-S4, Supporting Information).
HRMS-ESI(+): m/z calculated for C39H17F14N4O2 (M + H)+: 839.10380 found: 839.11078.
Yield: 63%
HRMS-ESI(+): m/z calculated for C39H17F14N4O2 (M + H)+: 839.10380 found 839.10869.
Yield: 22%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of graphene quantum dots with glycol corrole 2a
ce:section.ce:para: The functionalization of GQD_CO2H with corrole 2a via ester linkages was performed using EDAC to activate the carboxyl groups (see Fig. 1). No attempts were performed to incorporate corrole 2b into GQDs due to the lower yield obtained during its synthesis.

The GQD_CO2H functionalization with corrole 2a, was performed by adding 0.025 g of EDAC to 0.060 g of GQD_CO2H in 20 mL of DMSO. The resulting mixture was maintained under vigorous stirring for 2 h followed by addition of 0.020 g of corrole 2a, also in DMSO (5 mL). The mixture remained under stirring for 96 h at room temperature. The resulting conjugate was separated by filtration through a 0.1 μm membrane and subject to consecutive washings with water and dichloromethane to ensure the elimination of GQD_CO2H without covalently linked corrole units and free corrole 2a in solution. The final product could not be dispersed in water nor in dichloromethane that are good solvents for GQDs and corroles, respectively. The washes only ended when the presence of corrole 2a was not detected in the dichloromethane waste by UV–vis. The obtained material, which will be labelled as GQD_2a, was lyophilized affording 22 mg of product.

From the concentration of corrole 2a and that of the GQDs in a dispersion of GQD_2a, we estimate a ratio of 3 corrole units per GQD in the hybrid. The concentration of corrole in the dispersion was estimated using the molar absorption coefficient of the isolated corrole (6.5 × 105 M−1cm−1). The mass of GQDs in the same dispersion was estimated by subtraction of the mass of corrole from the total mass dispersed. The concentration of GQDs was estimated using for the molar mass of the GQDs a value of ≈72 kDa corresponding to the molar mass of a cylinder of graphite (ρ = 2.25 g/cm3) with 3.5 nm diameter and 5.5 nm height.
GQD_2a (22 mg): TGA: weight loss and temperature desorption (carboxylic acids and ester groups): 44.32%, 350–600 °C.
Raman: 1560 cm−1 (G band), 1361 cm−1 (D band); ID/IG = 1.7.

The GQDs were simulated by a graphene nanoplatelet of 1.8 nm diameter functionalized by a carboxylic group (GNP-COOH). In designing the GNP we chose to have a closed-shell ground state with an absorption spectrum that does not overlap with that of the corrole, and a large enough diameter to adsorb the corrole molecule, but still cost-efficient from the computational point of view. Calculations were performed on the isolated corrole 2a and GNP-COOH as well as on the GNP-COOH functionalized with the corrole 2a. The conformational search was performed by an automatic conformer generation procedure using metadynamics, the Conformer–Rotamer Ensemble Sampling Tool (CREST) [44], which uses a Semiempirical Quantum Mechanics (SQM) Hamiltonian [45,46]. The obtained conformations were also refined by DFT at the M06-2X-6-31 g(d,p) level, which was previously used to rationalize UV–vis and Raman spectra in polyaromatic stacked systems. Both methods yielded similar energetics. For comparison, two conformations were considered for the functionalized GNP-COOH, one with the corrole stacked on top the carbon core of the GNP and the other with the corrole alongside the carbon core. The UV–vis spectra and binding energies were computed at the M06-2X-water-6-31+g(d,p)/M06-2X-6-31g(d,p) level. The spectra were also computed at the M06-2X/6-31g(d,p), BMK-6-31g(d,p)/M06-2X-6-31g(d,p) and B3LYP-6-31g(d,p)/M06-2X-6-31g(d,p) levels yielding comparable results.

Human breast adenocarcinoma cells (MCF-7) were cultured and maintained at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% of penicillin/streptomycin. The cells were seeded on sterilized tissue culture treated wells (μ-Slide 8 well, glass bottom, Ibidi GmbH, Munich) for up to 24 h. Cells were passaged every 3–4 days. The day before labeling, cells were seeded in 8-well μ-slides glass bottom (Ibidi, Munich, Germany), final cell confluence ∼70%. For labelling, either a DMSO solution of corrole 2a (2 μl of 1 mg/mL) or a dispersion of GQD_2a (0.5 μl of 3 mg/mL) in DMSO was added to each well. The dilution where made so that the amount of DMSO in the cell medium did not exceed 1% to prevent toxicity effects due to DMSO. In some experiments, the nuclear stain Hoechst 33342 was also added to the well at a final concentration of 1–2 μM and incubated for 10 min at 37° C following instructions of the supplier (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nuclear Labelling Kit, catalogue number I34406). Upon incubation, cells were washed once with Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline. Fluorescence microscopy images were recorded using a laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica TCS-SP5) with a continuous Ar-ion laser (Multi-line LASOSs LGK 7872 ML05) and a Ti:sapphire laser (Spectra-Physics Mai Tai BB, 710–990 nm, 100 fs, 82 MHz). Typically, 80 × 80 μm images with 512 × 512 pixels where collected using a 63 × 1.2 N A. water immersion objective (HCX PL APO CS 63.0× 1.20WATERUV) with a scan speed of 200 Hz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQDs covalently linked to corrole units
ce:section.ce:para: The strategy envisaged to decorate GQDs with corrole units is summarized in Fig. 1 and required the previous synthesis of 5,10,15-tris(pentafluorophenyl)corrole 1 with a hydroxyethoxy unit and the functionalization of GQDs with additional carboxyl units to maximize the yield for the covalent integration of both structures.

The synthetic access to the glycol corrole involved the controlled nucleophilic substitution of one para fluorine atom of 5,10,15-tris(pentafluorophenyl)corrole 1 with ethylene glycol previously treated with sodium hydride in THF (Fig. 1). The reaction was performed in an oil bath at THF reflux temperature and after 8 h the TLC control showed an almost total consumption of the starting corrole accompanied by two significant fractions. After the work-up, purification and characterization the major fraction with a 63% yield was identified as the mono-substituted corrole 2a, while the minor fraction with 22% yield, was identified as the structural isomer 2b. The structures of these glycol corroles were confirmed by NMR, UV–vis and high-resolution mass spectrometry. In particular, from the analysis of 1H NMR spectra (Figure S1A and S2A) it was possible to assign, at lower field, all β-pyrrolic protons of the corrole core. In the aliphatic region, of each spectrum, two triplets at δ ∼4.1 ppm and δ ∼4.6 ppm, were identified as the resonances of the four protons of the glycol chain. The analysis of 19F NMR spectra (Figure S1B and S2B) confirmed the loss of one p-fluorine atom. Corrole 2a, was selected to be covalently linked to the carboxylated GQDs (GQD_CO2H), since it was isolated in much higher yield than 2b. The GQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal treatment of GO in the presence of NH4OH and H2O2 [6], and their extra functionalization with carboxylic groups was performed with chloroacetic acid (Fig. 1) [42]. The esterification of the carboxylic groups of the GQD_CO2H with the hydroxyl unit in corrole 2a, was performed in the presence of EDAC as shown in Fig. 1. After the coupling reaction the resulting solid was filtered and thoroughly washed with adequate solvents to remove any unreacted corrole unit.
The first evidence of the successful covalent functionalization of GQD_CO2H with corrole 2a was obtained from the comparison of FTIR spectra.

Fig. 2 shows the FTIR spectra of GQDs, corrole 2a and GQD_2a. The FTIR spectrum of the starting GQDs shows the expected presence of oxygenated functional groups on their surface, and in addition some amine and amide groups. Absorptions at 1335 and the shoulder at 1710 cm−1 can be assigned to C-O and CO bonds of unionized carboxylic groups, respectively; whereas the broad peaks centered at 3430 and 3150 cm−1 revealed N-H and O-H bond stretching vibrations. Most of the carboxylic groups are ionized as revealed by the strong bands at 1590 and 1400 cm−1 due to the antisymmetric and symmetric stretching of the carboxylate. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1590 cm−1 has also contributions from the stretching of the CC bonds and the band in the 1490-1359 cm−1 region have also contributions from the C-N stretching (amine/amide, 1400/1450 cm−1) and the in plane C-OH bending mode of the carboxylic group [47,48]. Upon carboxylation, the absorption of carboxyl group and hydroxyl group in the GQD_CO2H became more intense, as evidenced by the COO− symmetric stretching and antisymmetric stretching vibration around 1400 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, respectively (Figure S5) [49]. In the spectrum of corrole 2a the stretching vibrations at 3320 cm−1(NH pyrrole), 2926 cm−1 (CH pyrrole) and 1360 cm−1(CN) were attributed to the core of macrocycle. The gycol units originate sharp bands at 1037 cm−1 (C–O–C symmetric axial deformation), 1163 cm−1 (C–O–C asymmetric axial deformation) and 1535 cm−1 (CH deformation) [50]. In comparison with the FTIR spectrum of the GQDs, the spectrum of the hybrid GQD_2a shows a significant increase in the intensity of the CO stretching vibration band at 1713 cm−1 due to formation of the ester bond with corrole 2a [51–54]. These results shows that the corrole 2a was covalently linked to GQDs through novel ester bonds.

The covalent functionalization of GQDs with glycol corrole 2a, via the formation of ester linkage, was further confirmed by Raman (Fig. 3 ). GQDs Raman spectrum exhibit two prominent peaks at about 1374 and 1598 cm−1, which correspond to the characteristic D and G bands of carbon materials, respectively. The G band is a result of in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, whereas the D band is a ring breathing mode allowed only when the symmetry is broken by structural defects [55,56].

The Raman spectrum of GQD_CO2H is shown in the supporting information (Figure S6). The introduction of extra carboxylic groups on the structure of initial GQDs caused no distinct changes in the Raman spectrum as expected, since it results from the simple conversion of -OH groups to -CO2H. On the other hand, in the case of the GQD_2a hybrids, the D and G bands appeared at 1361 and 1562 cm−1 respectively, shifted to lower wavenumbers when compared to the GQDs. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band (ID/IG) in the GQDs is 1.9, which means that the average sp2 cluster size in the GQDs is about 2 nm [57]. Upon covalent conjugation with the corrole, the ID/IG decreases to 1.7. This small decrease of ID/IG suggests that the covalent modification of GQDs does not introduce a significant additional disorder in the graphene core of the novel hybrid material [58–60]. Based on the Tuinstra and Koening relationship (TK), a decrease of the ID/IG indicates a healing of the defects in the hexagonal carbon network due to reduction, which increases the average sp2 cluster size [61] This interpretation agrees with the fact that the corrole is attached to the GQDs through the carboxylic groups present on their surface, via pre-existent defects. However, it has been recently shown that the ID/IG reaches a maximum for small cluster sizes (2–3 nm). Thus, a given ID/IG may correspond to two different cluster sizes. The reason for this behavior is that the D-mode strength is not only proportional to the number of defects, which decrease with the cluster area, but it also depends on the probability of finding a six fold ring in the cluster, which increases with the cluster area. Depending on which side of the maxima we are looking at, the ID/IG can either increase or decrease with the cluster size. Thus, a decrease in the ID/IG ratio upon functionalization can also indicates a reduction of the cluster area, which is exactly the opposite of what is expected based on the TK relationship.

The TGA measurements performed under inert atmosphere also confirm the successful functionalization of GQDs with corrole 2a. Fig. 4 shows the thermal decomposition of GQD_CO2H, GQD_2a and corrole 2a. The TGA profile of GQD_CO2H exhibits a weight loss in the range of 200–600 °C (weight loss of 48%) that can be attributed to the decomposition of carboxylic acid groups that are present at the edges of the carbon dots structure. As it is expected, this initial weight loss in the GQD_CO2H was higher than in the GQDs (Figure S7). For GQD_ 2a and corrole 2a an additional weight loss was observed between 350 and 450 °C due to the removal of the ester moieties [62,63]. The tetrapyrrolic skeleton decomposes between 500 and 700 °C [31,64].

XPS characterization was carried out to confirm the presence of functional groups on the surface of GQD_2a, and the viability of esterification (Fig. 5 , Figure S8). Atomic percentage calculated for GQDs, corrole 2a and GQD_2a and the analysis of chemical binding ratios based on the integrated peak intensities are shown in Table S2 (supporting information). From the full-scan XPS spectrum of GQD_2a C, N and F were detected with peaks at 285.93 eV (C1s), 399.34 eV (N1s) and 687.52 eV (F1s), respectively (Figure S8). The appearance of F1s peak, which was absent in the spectrum of the pristine GQDs, supports the successfully incorporation of the glycol corrole into GQDs. The XPS spectrum of C1s of GQD_2a, was deconvoluted into three major components corresponding to CC/C-C at 284.55 eV, C-OH/C-O-C/C-N at 285.93 eV and CO/CN at 287.41 eV (Fig. 5) [65]. From the GQDs to GQD_2a it was observed not only a decrease in the intensity of the band assigned to the epoxy (C-O-C) and hydroxylic (C-OH) groups (285.93 eV) but also an increase in intensity of the band due to the carbonyl (CO) functionality (287.41 eV). Upon deconvolution of the N1s region, two peaks centered at 399.28 eV and 401.66 eV were assigned to N-H and CN functionalities, which were also present in the case of corrole 2a [66]. The functionalization led to an increase of the atomic percentage of nitrogen mainly associated with an increase of the intensity of the amine (N-H) band.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were used to compare morphologies and the size distributions of the as-prepared hybrid GQD_2a versus GQDs (Fig. 6 ). In agreement with our previous report [6], the average size of the GQDs alone was estimated to be 3.5 nm (Fig. 6A). The average size of the GQD_2a hybrids is closer to 4 nm and their size distribution is considerably larger (Fig. 6D). The wider size distribution observed upon functionalization is due to the chemical heterogeneity of the GQDs that will lead to different coupling number of the corrole molecules at its surface. The AFM image showed that the maximum height of the GQD_2a is 5.5 nm, which means that the functionalization induced also an increase in the height that was 3.7 nm in the unbound GQDs (Figure S9).

In Fig. 7 the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the hybrid GQD_2a are shown and compared with those of GQDs and glycol corrole 2a. The GQDs exhibited a weak absorption at 254 nm assigned to the π–π∗ transition of the aromatic sp2 domain [6,67]. The absorption spectrum of corrole 2a in DMSO shows the Soret-type band at 434 nm and three Q-type bands centered at 541, 586 and 618 nm. The emission spectrum of the free GQDs in water upon excitation at 400 nm show a broad band between 420 and 750 nm with a fluorescence quantum yield Φ = 2%. The emission of the free corrole in DMSO show two bands centered at 634 and 652 nm, with a fluorescence quantum yield Φ = 5%, in accordance with previous results [22,23].

In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQD_2a hybrid show the characteristic unstructured absorption of GQDs together with the Soret (434 nm) and Q-bands (620 nm) of corrole 2a. The UV–vis spectra of GQD_2a appears to be additive with respect to that of the isolated components, with no major shifts observed in the bands of the corrole upon conjugation to the GQDs. This observation suggests that there is no strong interaction between the two moieties in the ground state. The overall fluorescence quantum yield of GQD_2a is ≈ 6% (excitation at 400 nm, integration in the 420–750 nm region). When the isolated contribution of GQDs and corrole 2a are considered, the two units have emission yields of 0.7% and 5.3%, respectively. This means that the emission yield of the corrole increases slightly and that of the GQDs decreases by about a factor of three, suggesting the existence of non-radiative energy transfer from the dots to the corrole. A similar trend is observed in the emission lifetimes, with a decrease in the lifetime of the GQDs from 1.8 ns to 1.6 ns and an increase in the lifetime of the Corrole 2a, from 3.6 to 4.4 ns (Figure S10), upon conjugation. However, no clear rise-time component in the decays of the indirectly excited corrole is observed nor a significantly higher contribution from a fast decay component is observed in the carbon dots. Thus, the quenching occurs on a shorter timescale compared with the measurement resolution (≈0.1 ns).
Based on the absorption spectra of corrole 2a and GQD_2a, it was estimated that there are three corroles per GQD in GQD_2a (see experimental details).

Computational calculations where performed to rationalize the experimental UV–vis spectra. The GQD_2a was modelled using a graphene nanoplatelet (GNP) bearing a corrole 2a unit on the edge covalently attached via an ester bond (Fig. 8 ). The isolated GQDs where modelled by the GNP bearing a carboxylic group on the edge (GNP-COOH). The size of the carbon core in GNP roughly matches that of the corrole allowing for a good interaction via π-π stacking. Four different systems were studied in silico, the isolated GNP-COOH and corrole 2a units, and two possible conformations of the hybrid GNP_2a where the corrole molecule is either stacked on the carbon core of the GNP (GNP_2a stacked in Fig. 8C) or it is aligned alongside the carbon core in a nearly co-planar arrangement (GNP_2a side in Fig. 8D).

The stacked geometry is the global minimum of the hybrid both in the gas phase and in a water continuum. The energy difference between the two conformations is 29 kcal/mol. The binding energy estimated as the difference in energy of a given GNP_2a conformer with respect to the sum of the energy of the isolated components (Ebinding = EGNP_2a - EGNP - Ecorrole_2a ) is 33 kcal/mol for the stacked conformer and 4 kcal/mol for the side conformer. The simulated absorption spectra (300–600 nm) of the GNP_ 2a hybrids are compared with that of the isolated units in Fig. 9 . For corrole 2a, two main peaks are observed in-line with experimental observations. We note that due to the reduced size of the carbon core (1.8 nm) the calculated spectrum for the GNP is not directly comparable with that of the GQDs (4 nm). In particular, the intense absorption band predicted for the GNP at 400 nm is not expected in the experimental spectra. Rather than looking at absolute position of the electronic transitions, we are interested in determining the shifts observed in the spectra of corrole 2a upon functionalization to infer about the conformation. The calculations predict a red-shift in the main absorption bands of the most stable GNP_2a stacked conformation, and an overall loss of intensity, when compared with the isolated GNP-COOH and corrole 2a units. On the other hand, the absorption spectrum of the GNP_2a side conformation is well defined by the sum of the spectra of the isolated GNP-COOH and corrole 2a units. The experimental absorption spectrum of the GQD_2a hybrid in solution does appear to be additive with respect to that of the isolated units. Thus, based on optical spectroscopy we must conclude that the most relevant features in the absorption and emission spectra are due to corrole units alongside the carbon core with no significant stacking observed. However, we should note that if a stacked conformation should exist it should be difficult to identify by optical spectroscopy, given the generally weak intensity predicted for the absorption spectra and the almost certain quenching of the emission due to the strong interaction with the carbon core [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular internalization and distribution in live animal cells
ce:section.ce:para: The internalization and distribution of GQD_2a and corrole 2a in live mammalian cells was evaluated in human breast adebocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7). The uptake of GQDs by the cells can be a slow process. Several groups are developing protocols to improve the slow uptake including the use of cell-penetrating peptides [68–70]. We performed incubation overnight (18 h) to clearly distinguish GQD_2a emission from the autofluorescence of viable mammalian cells. With shorter incubation time (15 min, data not shown) no cellular uptake of GQD_2a was observed. The need for a long incubation time suggests that the mechanism of GQD_2a internalization could be dependent on the activation of endocytic pathways. GQD_2a are possibly internalized primarily through caveolae-mediated endocytosis in accordance with observations made in MCF7 cell lines.

Fig. 10 shows fluorescence microscopy images collected in the xy and xz plane for MCF7 cells incubated with GQD_2a (7.5 μg/mL) during 24 h. The fluorescence of GQD_2a is split in two different regions, one corresponding to emission of the GQD subunit at 474–600 nm (green emission, panels A and E) and the other corresponding to the emission of corrole 2a at 600–730 nm (red emission, panels B and F) upon excitation at 458 nm. The nuclear stain Hoechst was also added to provide a good definition of the nucleus. The emission of the nuclear stain collected at 400–470 nm is shown in panels C and G. The overlays of the separated images are shown in panels D and H for the xy and xz planes, respectively. In the overlays, the yellow colour shows the regions where the emission of the corrole 2a and the GQDs overlap because both originate at the GQD_2a hybrids. The yellow emission shows very high intensity spots resembling vesicles that may result from endocytic uptake, which are clearly distinguished from a background of unspecific labelling. Some isolated red colour can also be observed in the overlay due to emission of corrole 2a alone, which indicates that there is still some unconjugated corrole in solution. The presence of unconjugated corrole 2a is possibly due to the hydrophobic interactions between the corrole and the GQDs. Control experiments that prevents a complete cleaning where performed with unlabelled cells to show that the emission of GQD_2a is much stronger than cell autofluorescence (Figure S11).

The one-photon and two-photon fluorescence image of MCF-7 cells incubated with GQD_2a are compared in Fig. 11 . The one-photon image (Fig. 11A) was collected upon excitation at 458 nm, while the two-photon image (Fig. 11B) was collected upon excitation at 760 nm. The intensity distribution is similar in both conditions, with the bright spots emerging from a background of unspecific labelling. Fig. 11B shows that we can collect images using near-infrared excitation, and emission in the red part of the visible spectra, which prevents scattering from biological tissues and allows for a longer penetration depth being more advantageous for bioimaging. The emission spectra recorded inside the cell have a good overlap with the spectra in solution (Fig. 11C). The observed differences can be mostly assigned to instrumental effects, such as detector sensitivity and correction factors used in the spectrophotometer to compensate for detector sensitivity. The fluorescence image of MCF7 cells incubated with corrole 2a alone is shown in supporting information (Figure S12), together with the fluorescence emission spectra in the cells. The emission of corrole 2a in the cells shows only the band at 634 nm previously observed in solution.

In this study the functionalization of carboxylated graphene quantum dots with glycol corroles through ester bonds is described. The structural and functional characterization by FTIR, Raman, XPS, UV–vis and electronic microscopy support the successfully covalent functionalization. The UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of the hybrid GQD_2a are additive with respect to the isolated corrole 2a and GQDs units. The in silico studies show that the π-π interactions favour a conformation where the corrole is stacked at the core of the GQDs. However, the optical spectroscopy properties observed show no evidence of the existence of such conformation in solution. We conclude that the observed spectroscopic features in the UV–vis absorption and emission spectra are due to a conformation where the corrole is aligned side-by-side with the carbon core. Notably, the GQDs lose their good water dispersibility upon functionalizaton with the corrole but they could be dispersed in a 1% DMSO in water solution, conditions suitable for animal cells uptake studies. The uptake of the hybrid GQD_2a was slow, possibly mediated by endocytic pathways. Its distribution inside the cells shows high intensity spots that resemble vesicles resulting from endocytic uptake. It is also demonstrated that two–photon fluorescence images of the hybrid GQD_2a can be collected using near-infrared excitation and emission in the red part of visible spectra which is particular useful considering the possibility of their use as fluorescent labels of biological samples due to the reduced scattering and the longer penetration depth of NIR light. In the future, we will continue to work with these hybrids to explore their potential use as sensors and photothermal and phototherapy agents in biological media.
